statement.markdown: Given a sorted array of integers and a target value, write a function to count the number of occurrences of the target value in the array. The array is sorted in ascending order and may contain duplicate values. The function should return the number of times the target value appears in the array. For example, if the input array is [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 4] and the target value is 3, the function should return 3. If the target value is 5, the function should return 0. The input array will contain at least 1 and at most 10000 elements. The target value will be an integer between 1 and 10000.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of the input will contain a single integer n, representing the number of elements in the array. The second line will contain n space-separated integers, representing the elements of the array. The third line will contain a single integer, representing the target value.
statement.outputFormat: The output will be a single integer, representing the number of occurrences of the target value in the array.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array
The target value to be searched in the array

examples.explanation: The target value 3 appears 3 times in the array.
The target value 5 does not appear in the array.

hints.text: You can take advantage of the fact that the array is sorted. Start by finding the first occurrence of the target value in the array.

Once you have found the first occurrence of the target value, you can use the fact that the array is sorted to find the last occurrence of the target value. Think about how you can use the sorted property to your advantage.
The sorted property of the array means that all occurrences of the target value will be in a contiguous block. You can use this to your advantage to find the count of the target value.
You can use a binary search approach to find the first and last occurrence of the target value. This will allow you to take advantage of the sorted property of the array.

The binary search approach can be used to find the first and last occurrence of the target value, and then the count of the target value can be calculated as the difference between the last and first occurrence plus one.

editorial.title: Count Occurrences in Sorted Array
editorial.intuition: The problem requires counting the occurrences of a target value in a sorted array. A straightforward approach would be to iterate through the array and count the occurrences, but this would be inefficient for large arrays. A more efficient approach is to use binary search to find the first and last occurrences of the target value.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves iterating through the array and counting the occurrences of the target value. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and is inefficient for large arrays.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves using binary search to find the first occurrence of the target value, then iterating through the array from that point to count the occurrences. However, this approach is still not optimal as it involves iteration.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using binary search to find both the first and last occurrences of the target value. This approach takes advantage of the fact that the array is sorted, allowing for efficient searching using binary search. The time complexity of this approach is O(log n), making it much more efficient for large arrays.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that the binary search algorithm correctly finds the first and last occurrences of the target value. This is because the binary search algorithm divides the search space in half at each step, ensuring that the first and last occurrences are found efficiently.
editorial.dryRun: To test the optimal solution, a dry run can be performed using a sample array and target value. For example, using the array [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 4] and the target value 3, the optimal solution should return 3, indicating that the target value occurs 3 times in the array.
commonMistakes: Not taking advantage of the fact that the array is sorted
Using iteration instead of binary search
Not correctly handling edge cases, such as an empty array or a target value that does not occur in the array
Not optimizing the solution for large arrays
Not using a efficient data structure

functionSignature.java: public int solve(int[] nums, int target);
functionSignature.python: def solve(self, nums: list[int], target: int) -> int:
